topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an aggressive, buzzing sound that seemed to amplify every anxious beat in my chest. When Professor Albright called my name, the air immediately thickened, turning to cold, heavy sludge in my throat. I clutched the printed paper to my chest, the edges digging into my palms. Every word he read, every pointed question, felt like a physical assault. My hands started to tremble, a fine, uncontrollable vibration that made the ink on the page swim. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush that spread from my neck up to my hairline, making me acutely aware of every scrutinizing glance from my classmates. "Are you certain this is your own work, Alex?" he asked, his voice level, which only made the tremor in my hands worse. I managed a small, jerky nod, my gaze fixed desperately on the polished wood of the table, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I stared at the accusation on the screen, the words burning behind my eyes. It wasn't just the charge itself; it was the way everyone looked—the sudden, sharp withdrawal of attention, the way their shoulders seemed to tense up as they waited for my response. My stomach twisted into a tight, nauseous knot. I tried to speak, to construct a defense, but the sound caught in my throat, a dry, rasping thing. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving my skin feeling strangely cool and clammy. I focused on a small stain on the carpet, tracing its pattern with my eyes, trying to anchor myself to something real amidst the swirling chaos of judgment. "I... I don't understand," I finally managed, the words coming out thin and reedy, laced with an undeniable tremor that betrayed the depth of my distress.

[story 3]

The silence in the examination room was deafening, punctuated only by the scratch of pen on paper from the student next to me. When the professor finally laid out the evidence, a strange detachment washed over me, as if I were watching a poorly acted scene from a distance. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I looked down at my hands, noticing how tightly I was gripping my pen, the knuckles white against the plastic casing. A wave of profound disorientation hit me, making the room spin slightly. I wanted to retreat, to disappear into the shadows, but I remained rooted to my seat. "This is not fair," I whispered, the sound barely audible, a fragile thread of protest woven through the oppressive atmosphere.
